CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar May-27-2021 Set For Trial On Jan-24-22 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

DEMURRER to 1ST Amended CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-27-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: CROSS-DEFENDANT OSIRIS VENTURES, INC.'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS-COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Cross-Defendant Osiris (OVI)'s demurrer to the first amended cross-complaint ("FACC") is overruled.

The FACC sufficiently pleads the first cause of action for intentional misrepresentation and the second cause of action for false promise. The court in Reeder v. Specialized Loan Servicing LLC (2020) 52 Cal.App.5th 795 held that in pleading false promise claims, it "is insufficient to show an unkept but honest promise, or mere subsequent failure of performance." (Id. at 804 [citing Riverisland Cold Storage, Inc. v. Fresno-Madera Production Credit Assn. (2013) 55 Cal.4th 1169, 1183.].) Rather, "something more than nonperformance is required to prove the defendant's intent not to perform his promise." (Riverisland, 55 Cal.4th at 1183.) The FACC sufficiently alleges more than nonperformance. The FACC alleges that shortly after executing an amended letter of intent, OVI attempted to change the material terms and suggested in writing that it did not intend to perform as originally agreed. (FACC pars. 11, 12, 15.) These allegations are sufficient to create an inference that by attempting to renegotiate the deal after entering into it, OVI never intended to perform under the original terms promised. "To be sure, fraudulent intent must often be established by circumstantial evidence. Prosser, [Torts (5th ed. 1984) Sec. 109] for example, cites cases in which fraudulent intent has been inferred from such circumstances as defendant's insolvency, his hasty repudiation of the promise, his failure even to attempt performance, or his continued assurances after it was clear he would not perform." (Tenzer v. Superscope, Inc. (1985) 39 Cal.3d 18, 30.) Further, "[l]ess specificity is required when it appears from the nature of the allegations that the defendant must necessarily possess full information concerning the facts of the controversy." (See Committee On Children's Television, Inc. v. General Foods Corp. (1983) 35 Cal.3d 197, 217.) Finally, the FACC adequately pleads that cross-complainants sustained damages by foregoing other offers to purchase 99HT in justifiable reliance on OVI's representations. (FACC 17-20.) Whether cross-complainants can establish justifiable reliance raises questions of fact that cannot be resolved on demurrer. (Alliance Mortgage Co. v. Rothwell (1995) 10 Cal.4th 1226, 1239 ["Except in the rare case where the undisputed facts leave no room for a reasonable difference of opinion, the question of whether a plaintiff's reliance is reasonable is a question of fact." (citations and internal citations omitted)].)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/EPS)

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF OSIRIS VENTURES, INC.'s Application For Right To Attach Order, Order For Issuance Of Writ Of Attachment.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. Plaintiff and cross-defendant Osiris Ventures, Inc.'s application for a right to attach order is denied. Osiris and cross-complainant/defendant Higher Ground Management, LLC are litigating a dispute over the putative sale of a cannabis dispensary. Higher Ground says it took a $400,000 loan from Osiris as a "soft deposit" on the sale, then lost several million dollars when Osiris did not go through with the sale as promised. Based on the limited facts presented, I cannot make the required finding that Osiris "has established the probable validity" of its claims. (CCP Section 484.090(a)(2).) The loan is intertwined with the case's other issues and is not properly viewed in isolation.

Order signed.

MOTION FOR CHANGE OF VENUE
Off Calendar Aug-14-2020 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT HIGHER GROUND MANAGEMENT, LLC'S MOTION FOR CHANGE OF VENUE.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. Defendant Higher Ground Management, LLC's "motion to change venue" is denied. The case regards defendant's alleged nonpayment of a promissory note to be paid in San Francisco, making this court a proper venue. (CCP Sec 395.5; Anaheim Extrusion Co. v. Sup. Ct. (1985) 170 Cal.App.3d 1201.) Defendant says San Francisco is inconvenient for Los Angeles witnesses. However, if it is even timely to raise such issues at this stage, the facts pled indicate minimal need for discovery. Further, during the ongoing pandemic, most discovery and court appearances are done remotely, as are many trials. Thus, witness and attorney locations are largely irrelevant for the foreseeable future. No sanctions are awarded.

Order signed.
